description: Complete guide to doubly linked lists: node structure with prev pointer, insertion (beginning, end, position), deletion, traversal forward/backward, and advantages over singly linked lists.

sections.title: What is a Doubly Linked List?
sections.definition: A doubly linked list has nodes with two pointers: next (to next node) and prev (to previous node). Allows traversal in both directions.
sections.analogy: A two-way street: you can move forward to the next house or backward to the previous one.

sections.title: Node Structure and Helper Functions
sections.definition: Each node stores data, prev, and next. Provide functions to insert at front/back and print.

sections.title: Insertion at Beginning
sections.definition: Create new node, set its next to head, update head's prev if head exists, then set head to new node. O(1).

sections.title: Insertion at End
sections.definition: Traverse to last node, then attach new node. Update prev of new node and next of last node. O(n).

sections.title: Insertion at a Given Position
sections.definition: Handle beginning (pos 0) and middle. Maintain both prev and next links. O(n).

sections.title: Deletion of a Node
sections.definition: Given a pointer to node, adjust prev and next of neighbors, then delete. O(1) if node known. Delete by value: O(n) to find.

sections.title: Reversing a Doubly Linked List
sections.definition: Swap next and prev pointers for each node, and update head to last node. O(n).

sections.title: Advantages over Singly Linked List
sections.definition: Can traverse backwards, easier deletion of a given node (without traversing from head), but uses extra memory for prev pointer.

sections.title: Doubly Linked List as Deque
sections.definition: Doubly linked list allows O(1) insertion/deletion at both ends, making it suitable for deque (double-ended queue).
